name: Sandow
flavor: The circus strongman: feats of raw strength turned into a wall nothing gets past — plant your feet, brace the blow, and throw whatever's left standing.

proficiencies.skills: Choose two from Athletics, Intimidation, Perception, Performance, and Survival

parryReskin: You don't dodge a hit at all — you plant your feet, turn your shoulder or weapon into the line of the blow, and let your frame do what a lesser body couldn't. A Sandow's Parry is a brace, never a step back.
riposte: On a Full Dodge (your Parry d20 roll comes up strictly above your effective Parry DC), the braced impact drives the triggering attacker back: it is pushed 5 feet directly away from you, no save required, at no cost beyond the Parry itself — a shove, not a counterattack. On that same clean brace you may also spend 1 Grit to Brace Up: gain temporary hit points equal to your proficiency bonus + your Constitution modifier, lasting until the start of your next turn (like all temporary hit points these don't stack — a new Brace Up replaces any you already have). The shove is free; the Brace Up is the paid option.

engine.name: Grit
engine.description: A pool of raw physical fortitude and adrenaline built from being in the thick of a fight — trading blows, absorbing them, hurling anything not nailed down. The Sandow's sole resource. This baseline generation is shared by every Sandow from 1st level; your Strongman's Act (chosen at 3rd level) layers its own primary Grit stream on top of it — a defensive Act builds more from taking hits, an offensive Act more from dealing them, and Human Cannonball from impacts and forced movement.
engine.generation: Gain 1 Grit the first time each turn you hit a creature with a melee attack, AND gain 1 Grit the first time each turn you are hit by an attack — two separate sources, each capped at once per turn (landing a hit and being hit in the same turn grants 2 Grit total; a third hit landed or taken that same turn grants no further Grit from that source). This works from 1st level, before any subclass is chosen.
engine.spend: Spend Grit in 1-point increments to fuel Heave, Brace, and the other feats of strength described in your features. Unspent Grit carries over between rounds but drains to 0 if you finish a short or long rest without spending it.

features.name: Grit
features.description: You gain the Grit pool, a resource built from being in the fray — landing blows and taking them — and spent to fuel your feats of strength. See your Grit engine for exactly how it is generated, spent, and capped.

features.name: Mighty Build
features.description: You count as one size larger than your actual size for the purpose of carrying capacity, and for determining whether you can grapple, shove, or throw a creature — treat a Large creature as though it were Medium, a Huge creature as though it were Large, and so on. This extends to Heave below: you can grapple and throw creatures up to one size larger than the normal rules would otherwise allow.

features.name: Iron Grip
features.description: When you hit a creature no larger than Mighty Build allows with a melee attack, you can immediately try to grab it — no extra action or attack, it rides the same hit. The target must succeed on a Strength saving throw against your save DC (8 + your proficiency bonus + your Strength modifier) or be caught: while held it is grappled by you (its speed becomes 0) and has disadvantage on attack rolls it makes against you — pinned against your own frame, its swings at you go wide (its attacks against other creatures are unaffected). The hold has no time limit — the creature does not slip free on its own. It stays grabbed until you cash the hold in by Crushing it (see Crushing Grip) or Heaving it, until you let go or are incapacitated, or until it escapes on its turn by using its action to make a Strength (Athletics) or Dexterity (Acrobatics) check against your save DC. When a creature escapes this way it shakes free entirely and can't be caught by your Iron Grip again until the end of its next turn — so you can't chain-grab the same target, though you're free to set your hands on a different one in the meantime. (Ending the hold yourself by Crushing or Heaving the creature does not grant it this reprieve.) You can hold only one creature in your Iron Grip at a time — catching a new target releases the last. A creature held this way counts as grappled by you, so it is a valid target for Heave. Forced movement you cause with your own features (such as a Juggernaut's Bulldozer Charge) never breaks this hold — the creature is dragged with you rather than shoved free.

features.name: Heave
features.description: You grab something heavy — a creature you have grappled, or an object you're holding like a bench, a barrel, or a cage bar — and hurl it. This is used in place of one of your attacks when you take the Attack action (so at 1st level it replaces your single attack; with Extra Attack you can Heave and still swing). Spend 1 to 3 Grit: the thrown target flies 10 feet per point of Grit spent in a direction of your choice and lands in the nearest unoccupied space. What happens on landing depends on the target:

options.name: Unwilling creature or object
options.effect: A creature you already have grappled (Large or smaller, per Mighty Build), or a heavy object. It takes [[1d6]] bludgeoning damage per point of Grit spent and is knocked prone. No save — you already won the grapple to be holding it, so the throw simply happens.

options.name: Willing ally ("fastball special")
options.effect: No damage and no prone. You place the ally in an unoccupied space within range that has a clear path from you (not through walls or closed doors), ready to act — a launch, not an attack.

features.name: Hold the Line
features.description: When a hostile creature within your reach makes a melee attack against a creature other than you, you can use your reaction to make one melee weapon attack against the attacker. Additionally, whenever a hostile creature within your reach moves in a way that provokes an opportunity attack from you and you hit it with that opportunity attack, its speed becomes 0 for the rest of the current turn — it's stopped where it stands instead of completing its move. Hold the Line draws on the same single reaction as Parry and any other reaction-costed feature you have: punish an attacker or a fleeing creature, or hold your reaction in reserve to Parry — never both on the same round. This is not mind control: a creature is never forced to act against its will, only stopped or made to pay for what it already chose to do.

features.name: Brace
features.description: When you take damage from an attack — including one you failed to Parry, or one you had no reaction available to Parry — you can spend Grit, in 1-point increments and at no action cost, to reduce that damage by 3 per point of Grit spent, applied after any Parry multiplier and before the damage is otherwise resolved. Because Brace costs no action or reaction, it stacks with whatever reaction-costed response (or lack of one) you used for the same hit, including a round where your reaction already went to Hold the Line or a Parry. You can spend at most 2 Grit against a single instance of damage (reducing it by at most 6), and Brace cannot reduce damage below 0.

features.name: Crushing Grip
features.description: Your hold is a weapon in its own right. While you have a creature caught in your Iron Grip, two things become true. First, the grip turns mobile and sticky: you can drag the held creature with you when you move (it moves with you at no extra cost, staying grappled), and it has disadvantage on any check it makes to escape your grip. Second, you can crush it: in place of one attack when you take the Attack action, spend 1 Grit to deal [[1d6]] bludgeoning damage plus your Strength modifier to the held creature — no attack roll, the grip crushes down and the damage simply lands. Crushing spends the hold: once you Crush a creature it is no longer grabbed (you wring it out and shove it down), so to lock it again you must catch it fresh with a new hit. With Extra Attack you can Crush with one attack and make a normal attack with the other to re-catch it, so a target barely leaves your hands.

features.name: Extra Attack
features.description: You can attack twice, instead of once, whenever you take the Attack action on your turn.

features.name: Earthshaker
features.description: As an action, spend 3 Grit to slam a weapon, fist, or heavy object into the ground and send a shockwave through every creature of your choice within 10 feet of you. Each must succeed on a Strength saving throw (DC = 8 + your proficiency bonus + your Strength modifier) or be pushed 5 feet directly away from you and knocked prone. Earthshaker deals no damage on its own — it's forced movement and control, a battlefield reset, not another source of hurt. You can use Earthshaker once per turn.

keyStats.label: Save DC
keyStats.note: Save DC for effects your features force (e.g. Heave, Earthshaker).

keyStats.label: Grit Cap
keyStats.note: Most Grit you can hold at once.
